GCE A LEVEL MARKING SCHEME

  PREDICTED PAPER – MODERATE

           A LEVEL
   ECONOMICS – COMPONENT 1
           A520U10-1
INTRODUCTION
This marking scheme is designed to be used with the predicted Paper 1 (Moderate). It has been
prepared in the style of the WJEC Eduqas marking schemes, with Level-based mark grids for
extended questions and indicative content for all questions.

GENERAL MARKING GUIDANCE
Positive Marking
Credit should be given for what the learner writes, rather than penalising omissions. A very good
response can achieve full marks; a very poor response can achieve zero marks. The indicative
content suggests the range of ideas – it is not exhaustive, and learners do not need to include all
indicative content to reach the highest level.

21.   Using the diagram, explain why the firm maximises profit at output      Total
      Q₁.

      AO1: 2 marks                                                             3
      Award 2 marks for good knowledge and understanding that profits
      are maximised where MC = MR, with clear reference to the diagram
      showing the intersection.
      Award 1 mark for limited understanding of the profit-maximisation
      condition. The diagram is not used, or is used incorrectly.

      AO3: 1 mark
      Award 1 mark for analysis of why profit is maximised at MC = MR,
      with a clear chain of reasoning linking the condition to the diagram.

      Indicative content:
      • At output below Q₁, MR > MC, so producing an additional unit
      adds more to revenue than to cost, increasing total profit.
      • At output above Q₁, MC > MR, so producing more adds more to
      cost than to revenue, reducing total profit.
      • Therefore profit is maximised where MC = MR (Q₁), with price
      read off the AR/D curve at P₁.
      • Candidates may identify the profit area: (P₁ − AC at Q₁) × Q₁.
22. Consider the extent to which a cut in Bank Rate from 5% to 2% would be beneficial for the
economy. Adapt the diagram as part of your answer. (6 marks)

   Band            AO1 (2 marks)                 AO3 (2 marks)                 AO4 (2 marks)

       2     2 marks – Good                 2 marks – Good analysis      2 marks – Good evaluation
             knowledge                      Strong chain of reasoning    Strong counter-arguments,
             Good knowledge that a          explaining the               e.g. risk of demand-pull
             lower Bank Rate reduces        transmission mechanism:      inflation if economy near
             the cost of borrowing and      lower rates → lower cost     capacity; weak
             return on saving,              of borrowing, higher asset   transmission if confidence
             stimulating C and I,           prices, weaker exchange      is low (liquidity trap); time
             shifting AD to the right.      rate → higher C, I and (X–   lags; dependence on
             Clear link to the effect on    M) → AD shifts right →       shape of AD and slope of
             SRAS/LRAS and price            higher real GDP and lower    SRAS; depreciation could
             level. Correct adaptation of   unemployment in the short    raise import prices.
             the diagram showing AD         run.
             shifting right, with price
             level and output changes.

       1     1 mark – Limited               1 mark – Limited analysis    1 mark – Limited
             knowledge                      A chain of reasoning is      evaluation
             Some knowledge of how a        present but                  Evaluation is superficial or
             rate cut affects AD, but       underdeveloped or            lacks detail.
             with gaps. Diagram has         incomplete.
             significant missing
             elements or errors.

       0         0 marks – No valid             0 marks – No valid           0 marks – No valid
                    knowledge                       analysis                    evaluation

Indicative content:
• A 3 percentage-point cut in Bank Rate lowers the cost of borrowing for households and firms,
encouraging consumption (especially of durables and housing) and investment.
• This shifts AD to the right. In the short run, with spare capacity (Y₁ < YFE), real GDP rises towards
YFE and unemployment falls, with only modest upward pressure on the price level.
• A weaker currency (through reduced 'hot money' inflows) raises export competitiveness, further
boosting AD via (X – M).
BUT:
• If the economy is already near full employment, AD shifting right mainly raises the price level
(demand-pull inflation) with little real output gain.
• The effectiveness depends on confidence – if households and firms are pessimistic, the multiplier
may be weak (similar to a liquidity trap).
• Time lags of 12–18 months for monetary policy to take full effect.
• A weaker pound raises imported inflation, especially for energy and food.
• Low rates over a long period may inflate asset price bubbles and encourage reckless lending.
• Allow any other sensible evaluations.
23.   With the aid of a diagram, comment on the likely price elasticity of   Total
      supply of Norwegian oil in the short run.

      AO1: 2 marks                                                            3
      Award 2 marks for good understanding of PES. Accurate diagram
      showing inelastic supply (steep SR supply curve).
      Award 1 mark for limited understanding. Diagram inaccurate or
      missing.

      AO2: 1 mark
      Award 1 mark for effective use of the stimulus (lead time, capital
      intensity, infrastructure requirements) to support the argument that
      supply is price inelastic.

      Indicative content:
      • Oil extraction has very long lead times (5–10 years from discovery
      to production) and requires highly capital-intensive infrastructure.
      • Spare capacity at existing rigs is limited; firms cannot quickly
      increase output in response to higher prices.
      • This makes short-run PES highly inelastic (%ΔQs < %ΔP);
      diagram shows a steep supply curve where a large rise in price
      from P₁ to P₂ produces only a small rise in quantity from Q₁ to Q₂.
      • In the long run, PES becomes more elastic as new rigs can be
      built.
24.   Explain the shape of this firm's short run total cost curve.             Total

      AO1: 2 marks                                                              4
      Award 2 marks for good understanding that the TC curve reflects
      fixed costs at zero output, increasing returns to the variable factor
      at low output, and diminishing returns at higher output.
      Award 1 mark for limited understanding with gaps or confusion;
      limited use of the diagram.

      AO3: 2 marks
      Award 2 marks for a strong chain of reasoning linking the TC shape
      to the underlying economic concepts.
      Award 1 mark for a chain of reasoning that is unconvincing or lacks
      detail.

      Indicative content:
      • At zero output, the firm still incurs fixed costs of £500 (rent,
      equipment depreciation) – TC intercepts the vertical axis at £500,
      not the origin.
      • As output initially rises, TC grows relatively slowly due to
      increasing marginal returns to the variable factor (e.g. labour);
      specialisation and division of labour allow efficient use of the fixed
      factor (ovens).
      • At higher output levels, TC rises more steeply because of
      diminishing marginal returns – the fixed factor (ovens, workspace)
      becomes overused, and additional workers add progressively less
      to output.
      • The characteristic 'S-shaped' TC curve: shallow slope at low
      output → steeper slope at high output.
25. With reference to the matrix and economic theory, discuss whether or not governments
should coordinate climate policy internationally. (8 marks)

  Band          AO2 (3 marks)               AO3 (2 marks)               AO4 (3 marks)

    3      3 – Excellent application                             3 – Excellent evaluation
           Matrix well used; both A                              Strong counter-argument using
           and B's dominant                                      economic theory (game theory,
           strategies identified;                                public goods, free-riding).
           employment numbers                                    Genuinely balanced
           referenced explicitly.                                judgement.

    2      2 – Good application        2 – Good analysis         2 – Good evaluation
           Matrix used; both A and     Strong line of argument   Strong counter-argument; use
           B's strategies discussed.   for why international     of economic theory more
                                       coordination is in        limited.
                                       principle beneficial.

    1      1 – Limited application     1 – Limited analysis      1 – Limited evaluation
           Incomplete or inaccurate    Chain of reasoning        Counter-arguments present but
           understanding of the        present but               underdeveloped.
           matrix.                     underdeveloped.

    0                  0                           0                           0

The grid assumes the main line is in favour of coordination, but the answer is fully reversible.

Indicative content:
AO2:
• For Country A, 'No Carbon Tax' is a dominant strategy: if B taxes, A gains +40 (vs −10); if B does
not tax, A gains +5 (vs −50).
• For Country B, 'No Carbon Tax' is also a dominant strategy: if A taxes, B gains +30 (vs −15); if A
does not tax, B gains +10 (vs −40).
• The Nash equilibrium is (No Tax, No Tax) yielding (+5, +10) – but the jointly optimal outcome might
be (Tax, Tax) at (−10, −15), which avoids much larger environmental costs not shown in the matrix.
• This is a classic Prisoner's Dilemma.
AO3 (if in favour of coordination):
• Climate is a global public good; the marginal social benefit of emission reductions is not captured by
individual countries → free-riding problem.
• Without coordination, each country has an incentive to defect (tragedy of the commons), leading to
sub-optimal emissions.
• Coordinated policy (e.g. Paris Agreement, EU ETS) corrects this by aligning private with social
incentives.
• Reduces 'carbon leakage' where firms relocate to countries without carbon pricing.
AO4 (if against / evaluation):
• Game theory shows non-cooperation is the Nash outcome – enforcement is very difficult;
international agreements lack binding penalties.
• Developing countries may argue coordinated policy is unfair – they have contributed less to
historical emissions.
• Different countries have different marginal abatement costs; a uniform policy may be inefficient.
• National circumstances matter: coal-dependent economies face higher adjustment costs.
• Repeated-game logic: with ongoing interactions, cooperation is more likely than the matrix (a single-
shot game) suggests.
• Allow any other sensible point.
26. With reference to the data above, consider how effective the UK Government's sugar tax
has been in correcting market failure. (8 marks)

  Band           AO2 (3 marks)              AO3 (2 marks)                  AO4 (3 marks)

    3       3 – Excellent                                         3 – Excellent
            Both numerical (obesity                               Strong counter-argument that
            rates, NHS cost,                                      the tax may not correct market
            reformulation %) and                                  failure / may cause government
            textual data used well.                               failure, with strong use of
                                                                  theory.

    2       2 – Good                   2 – Good                   2 – Good
            Numerical and textual      Strong line of argument    Strong counter-argument;
            data used.                 for tax effectiveness.     theory more limited.

    1        1 – Limited application      1 – Limited analysis          1 – Limited evaluation

    0                   0                          0                              0

Indicative content:
AO2:
• Adult obesity has continued to rise (22% → 26% between 2010 and 2023) despite the tax,
suggesting limited direct impact on BMI outcomes.
• However, sugar content in taxed drinks has fallen by 43% – a significant supply-side effect through
reformulation.
• Revenue of £300m can fund health education programmes.
• NHS cost of £6.5bn indicates substantial external costs.
• Regressive impact: deprived children twice as likely to be obese; sugar tax may fall more heavily on
lower-income households who spend a larger share on sugary drinks.
AO3:
• Sugary drinks involve negative externalities of consumption (NHS costs imposed on taxpayers) and
information failure (long-term health effects underestimated by consumers).
• An indirect tax internalises the externality, raising the private cost towards the social cost and
reducing consumption to the socially optimal level (Qs → Q*).
• The 43% reformulation suggests the tax has successfully changed producer behaviour, reducing
sugar intake even without reducing consumption volume.
• Welfare gain: triangle area between MSC and MPC up to original Qeq is recovered.
AO4:
• Obesity is caused by many factors (sedentary lifestyles, calorie-dense foods, portion sizes); a sugar
tax alone cannot solve it.
• PED for sugary drinks may be inelastic, so quantity response is limited.
• Regressive nature raises equity concerns.
• Substitution to other high-calorie products not covered by the tax.
• Government failure risk: unintended consequences; information asymmetry about 'correct' tax rate.
• However, reformulation effect is sustained and the tax has been revenue-positive; more accurate to
see this as a partially successful intervention requiring complementary policies (education, exercise
promotion).
27. Using the information above and relevant economic theory, discuss the extent to which
investment in education is the most effective way to promote economic development. (8
marks)

  Band           AO2 (3 marks)              AO3 (2 marks)                AO4 (3 marks)

    3       3 – Excellent application                            3 – Excellent evaluation
            All columns used well to                             Strong counter that education
            support arguments.                                   is not sufficient or not the most
                                                                 effective; good use of theory.

    2         2 – Good application         2 – Good analysis           2 – Good evaluation

    1              1 – Limited                1 – Limited                   1 – Limited

    0                   0                          0                             0

Indicative content:
AO2:
• Some correlation between adult literacy and HDI: Botswana (88%, HDI 0.693) vs Ethiopia (52%,
HDI 0.498).
• But not perfect: DR Congo has 80% literacy yet the lowest HDI (0.457).
• Life expectancy alone does not predict HDI – Rwanda has the highest LE (70) but HDI below
Kenya.
• GDP per capita is highly correlated with HDI (Botswana highest in both).
• Ghana and Kenya have similar literacy (80% vs 83%) but very different GDP per capita.
AO3 (in favour of education):
• Education raises human capital → higher labour productivity → higher long-run growth and
incomes.
• Education generates positive externalities: lower birth rates, better health outcomes, reduced crime.
• Reduces information failure and corruption over time.
• Enables structural transformation from agriculture to manufacturing and services.
• A key component of the HDI itself, so investment in education mechanically improves the HDI.
AO4 (evaluation):
• DR Congo's experience shows high literacy alone is not sufficient – institutional quality and political
stability matter more.
• Alternative policies may be more effective: health investment (cf. Rwanda's LE gains), infrastructure,
governance reform, trade liberalisation.
• Education requires complementary inputs: qualified teachers, textbooks, physical infrastructure – all
costly.
• Brain drain can offset benefits – educated workers may emigrate to HICs.
• Returns to education depend on quality of jobs available – graduate unemployment is high in many
LEDCs.
• Timescales: education investment takes 10–20 years to fully materialise; other policies may have
faster effects.
• Aid, trade, debt relief and FDI also play important roles.
